coredata.dc:title: One-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of blue emissive multifunctional N-S-P co-doped carbon dots as a nanoprobe for sequential detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid in real samples, fluorescent ink and logic gate operation
coredata.dc:creator: Laddha, Harshita
Yadav, Priya
Jain, Yachana
Sharma, Manish
Reza, Mohtashim
Agarwal, Madhu
Gupta, Ragini
coredata.dc:description: In this communication, blue light-emitting fluorescent N-S-P co-doped carbon dots (PGP-CDs) were synthesized by a simple, yet rapid procedure using microwave charring. Low cost, easily accessible precursors like glutathione, polyethylene glycol (PEG-400), and o-phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were used in the synthesis following which PGP-CDs obtained with a good quantum yield (15%) were well characterized. The HRTEM analysis showed that PGP-CDs were well distributed and spherical, with an average size of 4.5 nm. XPS analysis confirmed the chemical composition of doped N-S-P-CDs, and fluorescence spectroscopy revealed that they exhibit pH-independent and excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima at 380/455 nm. Blue fluorescence of the PGP-CDs was exclusively quenched by the addition of carcinogenic Cr(VI) in the presence of other metal ions within a linear range of 0.1–100 µM which can be explained by the inner filter effect (IFE). The regeneration of carbon dots could be achieved by the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) and thus, PGP-CDs act as a sensitive fluorescent turn “on–off-on” nano-sensor in water. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) was found to be 4 nM which is lesser than the permissible limit (0.9 µM) as prescribed by World Health Organisation (WHO) in drinking water. PGP-CDs show reversibility up to four cycles and on this basis, a combinatorial molecular logic gate consisting of a special combination of logic functions NAND & NOT has been constructed. Furthermore, PGP-CDs were also utilized as fluorescent ink without any chemical alteration. For real sample applications, PGP-CDs were also successfully applied to determine Cr(VI) in real water samples and PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex to detect AA levels in food samples. Results obtained from real sample analysis suggest that PGP-CDs can be used as a potential nanoprobe that offers outstanding precision in detecting the trace amount of Cr(VI) and AA with a low detection limit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots
Cr(VI) quenching
Fluorescent turn “on-off-on” sensor
Luminescent ink
Logic gate function

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-S-P co-doped carbon dots
General method for the sensing of Cr(VI) and AA
Microstructural and morphological analysis of PGP-CDs
pH effect and stability of PGP-CDs
Fluorescence spectroscopic studies of PGP-CDs with metal ions:
Selectivity study of PGP-CDs towards Cr(VI)
Fluorescence spectroscopic studies of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex with various biomolecules
Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples
Detection of AA in fruits and vegetables

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H. Laddha is thankful to CSIR, New Delhi, for Junior Research Fellowship. P. Yadav acknowledges CSIR-UGC for the fellowship. M. Sharma acknowledges MNIT, Jaipur for financial assistance. The authors are grateful to the Materials Research Centre (MRC), MNIT Jaipur, for material characterization.

H. Laddha is thankful to CSIR, New Delhi, for Junior Research Fellowship. P. Yadav acknowledges CSIR-UGC for the fellowship. M. Sharma acknowledges MNIT, Jaipur for financial assistance. The authors are grateful to the Materials Research Centre (MRC), MNIT Jaipur, for material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PGP-CDs derived from PEG-400, glutathione, and o-phosphoric acid.

(a, b) TEM image of PGP-CDs; the inset of (a) displays the SAED pattern of the low magnification TEM image. (c) HRTEM image of PGP-CDs highlights with red circles showing the arrangements of graphitic packing as interplanar fringes. (d) its corresponding size distribution histogram. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Survey XPS of PGP-CDs. (b) Its elemental composition; High resolution scan for (c) C 1s. (d) N 1s. (e) P 2p. (f) O 1s. (g) S 2p.

(a) UV–vis spectrum and the insets display the photographic image of PGP-CDs in the daylight and the UV light. (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra (excited from 300 nm to 480 nm with the increment of 20 nm towards the higher wavelength).

(a) Emission spectra of PGP-CDs in the presence or absence of different metal ions (1 × 10-5 M) at λ ex: 380 nm. (b) Fluorescence quenching spectra of PGP-CDs at varying concentrations of Cr(VI) (0–400 µM). (c) Image of the PGP-CDs solution with various metal ions under the UV-light displaying the selectivity for Cr(VI) (turn-off).
Effect on the emission intensity of PGP-CDs with other metal ions in the presence of Cr(VI).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex displaying the regeneration of fluorescence intensity on the addition of AA selectively with different biomolecules (1 × 10-5 M) at λ ex: 380 nm. (b) Fluorescence titration spectra of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex upon gradual addition (0–350 µM) of AA. (c) Image displaying selectivity of AA and different biomolecules towards the restoration of fluorescence intensity of the quenched solution of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex.

(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PGP-CDs with absorption spectra of PGP-CDs and Cr(VI). (c,b) Schematic representation of plausible mechanism for PGP-CDs as switchable turn “on–off-on” fluorescent nanosensor for the sensing of Cr(VI) and AA.
Reversible fluorescent behavior of PGP-CDs extending to four cycles by the alteration of Cr(VI)/AA addition.
Photographic image showing the use of PGP-CDs as fluorescent ink under UV-light illumination.
Truth table and logic gate depiction depending on the fluorescence response of PGP-CDs towards Cr(VI) and AA as inputs and emission at 455 nm as output.
Determination of Cr(VI) in different water samples by PGP-CDs.
Determination of AA in food samples by PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex.

entry.bold: Found by the method developed (mg/100 g)
Found by the reference method (mg/100 g)

head.ce:title: One-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of blue emissive multifunctional N-S-P co-doped carbon dots as a nanoprobe for sequential detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid in real samples, fluorescent ink and logic gate operation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Malaviya National Institute of Technology Jaipur

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this communication, blue light-emitting fluorescent N-S-P co-doped carbon dots (PGP-CDs) were synthesized by a simple, yet rapid procedure using microwave charring. Low cost, easily accessible precursors like glutathione, polyethylene glycol (PEG-400), and o-phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were used in the synthesis following which PGP-CDs obtained with a good quantum yield (15%) were well characterized. The HRTEM analysis showed that PGP-CDs were well distributed and spherical, with an average size of 4.5 nm. XPS analysis confirmed the chemical composition of doped N-S-P-CDs, and fluorescence spectroscopy revealed that they exhibit pH-independent and excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima at 380/455 nm. Blue fluorescence of the PGP-CDs was exclusively quenched by the addition of carcinogenic Cr(VI) in the presence of other metal ions within a linear range of 0.1–100 µM which can be explained by the inner filter effect (IFE). The regeneration of carbon dots could be achieved by the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) and thus, PGP-CDs act as a sensitive fluorescent turn “on–off-on” nano-sensor in water. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) was found to be 4 nM which is lesser than the permissible limit (0.9 µM) as prescribed by World Health Organisation (WHO) in drinking water. PGP-CDs show reversibility up to four cycles and on this basis, a combinatorial molecular logic gate consisting of a special combination of logic functions NAND & NOT has been constructed. Furthermore, PGP-CDs were also utilized as fluorescent ink without any chemical alteration. For real sample applications, PGP-CDs were also successfully applied to determine Cr(VI) in real water samples and PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex to detect AA levels in food samples. Results obtained from real sample analysis suggest that PGP-CDs can be used as a potential nanoprobe that offers outstanding precision in detecting the trace amount of Cr(VI) and AA with a low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots have acquired an immense scientific interest in the last few decades as a novel form of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial [1,2]. Their unique properties, such as exceptional optical property [3], good biocompatibility [4], low toxicity [5], tremendous water solubility [6], robust chemical inertness [3], high photo and chemical stability [7], and ease of functionalization [8] as compared to those of organic fluorescent dyes and conventional semiconductor quantum dots [9], make them ideal candidates for their use in diverse fields [5,10–17]. Their popularity can be envisaged from the plethora of raw materials which have been reported in the literature for their preparation from natural sources like fruits [18,19], grass, plant [20], leaves, spices [21–24], etc. and synthetic sources like chemicals [25], synthetic polymers [26], etc. Moreover, numerous preparatory methods, ranging from simple pyrolysis, carbonization, and microwave irradiation to challenging procedures like laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, arc-discharge, thermal oxidation, etc. [8,25,27] have been reported for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs. Amongst them, microwave decomposition is rated as an efficient and rapid route for the fabrication of luminescent CDs owing to the on and off pulses of electromagnetic waves [12,28]. Recently, various studies have been proposed for doping heteroatoms into the framework of CDs which could be exploited further in several applications [8,29,30]. Doping with heteroatoms either single (N, S, P, and B)[31] or co-doping (N-P [32], N-S [33], N-B [34], and Mg/N [35]) modulates the intrinsic photo-physical properties of the carbon dots. For example, Zhao et al. developed nitrogen-doped CDs for detecting Fe3+ in water samples [36]. Bhati and their group reported nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped CDs as a potential photo catalyst for the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the wastewater [11]. Guo et al. revealed that boron and nitrogen co-doped CQDs can be utilized as fluorescent ink for scripting purposes on paper for anti-counterfeiting [14]. Incorporation of nitrogen, sulphur, and phosphorous heteroatoms not only promotes the coordinated binding interactions of CDs with multiple targets by enriching the electron-donation or acceptance ability of the carbon dots but also stimulates the redox reaction on the surface of CDs, thereby generating remarkable optical properties and expanding their possible applications [35,3738,39]. Hence, countless efforts were made for the production of a variety of CDs doped with heteroatoms having simple and economical one-step strategies.

The continuous discharge of toxic Cr(VI) into the environment through anthropogenic activities like electroplating, petroleum mining and refining, tanning of leather, and dyeing, etc. leads to relatively more serious health hazards than its low valent states like Cr(0) and Cr(III), owing to its carcinogenic and mutagenic properties [40,41]. According to WHO, the permissible limit of Cr(VI) in drinking water should be under 0.9 µM [42]. Therefore, it is pertinent to quantify it accurately at this low concentration from environmental samples of drinking water, industrial waste, and other chromium polluted areas. Numerous analytical techniques are available like colorimetry [43,44], chromatography [45], electrochemical method [46], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [47], and atomic absorption spectrometry [48] for the estimation of Cr(VI) but these methods require highly specialized personnel and expensive instruments with complicated sample pre-treatment. Therefore, the development of simple, cost-effective, extremely sensitive, and selective luminescent nanosensors for the rapid detection of Cr(VI) is in great demand. Moreover, ascorbic acid is a water-soluble vitamin that is abundantly distributed in foodstuff, and owing to its antioxidant properties, it is widely used to treat numerous ailments such as scurvy, flu, dementia, mental illness, cancer, cardiovascular disease, and AIDS [49,50]. Although various probes for the monitoring of Cr(VI) and AA [51–53] are reported in the literature, however, it has always been desirable to develop simpler and economic techniques.

In this paper, an eco-friendly one-step method is described for the fabrication of highly fluorescent, water-soluble N-S-P co-doped carbon dots through microwave-induced pyrolysis of a mixture of glutathione, polyethylene glycol, and o-H3PO4 (Scheme 1 ). Detailed investigation shows that PGP-CDs act as a switchable turn “on-off-on” nanosensor for sequential quantification of Cr(VI) and AA. PGP-CDs are pH-independent and exhibit tremendous stability and solubility in the aqueous media. PGP-CDs have excellent selectivity for the fluorometric detection of Cr(VI) ion among various metal ions in water at pH 7. To validate the practicability of this fluorescent nanoprobe in real water applications, various samples from tap water, drinking water, and discarded water from the textile industry were collected and experimented on to check the susceptibility of PGP-CDs for the detection of Cr(VI) ion. The results obtained were comparable to those found with the reference method i.e., 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method [54], and these outcomes suggest that the proposed method is notably efficient in the recognition of Cr(VI) in real water samples. Additionally, the realistic application studies of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex for the detection of AA levels in different fruits and vegetables were performed and the results obtained are in good agreement with the reference method i.e., o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) method [55] for the determination of AA. Therefore, the turn “on–off-on” behavior of PGP-CDs towards Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid illustrates the practical application of PGP-CDs as a reversible luminescent nano switch in the field of sensing and could be established as a promising platform for responsive and selective detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples. Furthermore, these CDs can be used in molecular logic gate operation and also as fluorescent ink.

Polyethylene glycol-400, reduced glutathione, and o-phosphoric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals as well as solvents purchased are of analytical quality and utilized without any additional purification. De-ionized (DI) water was utilized in all the experiments. Samsung Combi (CE104VD) domestic microwave oven was used for charring of precursors. Ultrasonication was done using Elma S 70H Ultrasonicator with 37 kHz output frequency. Remi R-12C Plus was utilized for centrifugation purposes. The size and morphology of PGP-CDs were characterized by High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) on a Tecnai 20 G2 200 kV, STWIN model instrument with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded using ESCA + omicron nanotechnology, Oxford Instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was done on a Bruker Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Alpha) using KBr pellets over the range of 500–4000 cm−1. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV‐1800 spectrophotometer in standard 3.5 ml quartz cells with 10 mm path length. All fluorescence spectra were performed in an aqueous medium with a Fluoromax 4C.L. Spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-S-P co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N-S-P co-doped carbon dots were synthesized using 0.406 g (1.5 mmol) of glutathione (source of C, N, S), 1 ml of PEG-400 (source of C), and 1 ml of H3PO4 (source of P) as readily available precursors via a simple, rapid and efficient method of microwave-induced pyrolysis. The amount and/or concentration of all the three precursors of PGP-CDs were optimized against fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1). The mixture was sonicated for 5 min in an ultrasonic bath and then microwaved in a household microwave oven (power 450 W) for three minutes, during which the transparent solution changed to a dark brown color indicating the carbonization of reactants. Upon cooling to room temperature, the crude product was blended with 10.0 ml DI water and a brown colored solution was obtained. The blended mixture was neutralized with ammonia for the removal of excess of phosphoric acid in the form of (NH4)3PO4 then, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Later, the obtained supernatant liquid was filtered off via a 0.22 mm membrane filter to discard the larger particles from the brown-colored mixture. Then, CDs were purified by column chromatography on a Sephadex G-100 gel filtration column eluted with water for separation. Further, the PGP-CD solution obtained was vacuum dried at 60 ◦C for 24 h resulting into a thick viscous product which was dispersed in water to form a homogenous solution with concentration of 0.35 mg/mL and stored in a refrigerator for future usage.

The well-known formula for the calculation of Quantum Yield (QY) can be represented by:
QY
S
=
QY
R
×
I S
I R
×
Abs
R
Abs
S
×
ϑ
S
2
ϑ
R
2
where QY, I, Abs, and υ are the quantum yield, integrated fluorescence intensity, optical absorbance, and refractive index of the respective solvents. S and R stand for the sample and reference used. Here, quinine sulphate was chosen as a reference (QY ~ 54%) which was prepared in 0.5 M sulphuric acid solution while PGP-CDs solution was prepared in DI water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General method for the sensing of Cr(VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensing study of Cr(VI) and AA with PGP-CDs (0.35 mg/mL) was done in DI water at room temperature. Stock solutions of salt of metal ions (1 × 10−3 M) and biomolecules (1 × 10−3 M) were prepared in water at pH 7 and later diluted. The fluorescence spectra were obtained after incubation of the solution for 1 min at room temperature. All fluorescence spectral measurements were measured at fixed excitation and emission slits (1.5 nm). The fluorescence emission spectra of PGP-CDs centered at 455 nm were recorded at 380 nm excitation wavelength.

To test the practicality of PGP-CDs, experiments were carried out for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples. For the quantification of Cr(VI), three different water samples viz. tap water, drinking water, and wastewater from the textile industry were collected. The discarded water from the textile industry was filtered through a filter paper and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove suspended particles. Before the detection, the pH value of all real water samples was adjusted to 7.0. The amount of Cr(VI) was also estimated using 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method [54] as a reference method. For detection of AA in real samples, different fruits (kiwi, papaya) and vegetables (lemon, tomato, and cucumber) were taken and their concentration was determined through the developed method. Briefly, for the extraction of AA content from real samples, the edible portion of the fruits and vegetables was accurately measured (50 g), then mixed with 50 ml DI, crushed, and centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered and diluted 5-fold and the concentration of the samples was determined by standard procedure [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural and morphological analysis of PGP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology of PGP-CDs were characterized using TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 1 a, b displays that PGP-CDs are sphere-shaped and are well dispersed. The size distribution histogram obtained from a statistical analysis of approximately 50 particles displays a Gaussian fitting curve with a narrow range of 2–7 nm and the average size is around 4.5 nm. Fig. 1c shows the HRTEM image of PGP-CDs, reveals that one PGP-CDs particle has a discernible lattice structure with an average lattice spacing of 0.279 nm. Also, the SAED pattern shows the crystalline nature of PGP-CDs (Fig. 1a-inset).

XPS is operated to explore the chemical composition and structure of the PGP-CDs. Fig. 2 displays the occurrence of carbon (~285.0 eV), nitrogen (~398.5 eV), sulfur (~163.0 eV), phosphorus (~130.3 eV) and oxygen (~531.0 eV) atoms [56]. The content of all the elements present is indicated in Fig. 2b. Further, the high-resolution C1s spectrum of PGP-CDs (Fig. 2c) is deconvoluted into five apparent peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.6 eV, and 288.6 eV, which are ascribed to C–C/CC, C–P/C–N, C–O–C (epoxy & alkoxy)/C–S, CN/CO, and O–CO, respectively [38,57]. The N1s spectrum has three peaks locating at 399.4 eV, 399.8 eV, and 400.8 which can be attributed to the C–N–C, N–(C)3, and N–H, respectively (Fig. 2d) [32,38]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum exhibit three peaks at 530.6 eV, 532 eV, and 532.9 eV (Fig. 2f), corresponding to C–O–C/C–OH, C–O, and CO/CN functional groups, respectively [53]. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 2g) indicates two peaks at 162.8 eV and 164 eV, which is in the agreement with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the –C–S– bond of the thiophene−S, owing to their spin–orbit couplings [37,58]. The deconvolutions of the P2p spectrum (Fig. 2e) into two distinct peaks at 132.7 eV and 133.5 eV, which are associated with P–O and P–C/P–N, respectively [53,58].

N-S-P doping and functional groups of the PGP-CDs were also explored by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. S2 shows the broad and fused absorption peaks at about ~3375 and ~3239 cm−1 correspond to –OH and –NH stretching vibrations [33]. The peak at ~2884 cm−1 is attributed toC−H stretching vibration [59]. A strong peak centered at ~1664 cm−1 is associated with the CO/CN stretching vibrations [53]. The peak at 1450 cm−1 shows the existence of CC stretching vibrations [56]. A peak at around ~1175 is ascribed to C−S. A characteristic absorption peak at ~1066 cm−1 refers to C−O−C/ P−O−C bonds [53,56].

PGP-CDs gives a strong absorbance in the UV- region with two peaks, one at 260 nm and a shoulder peak at 320 nm due to CC (π- π*) and CO (n- π*) respectively [3,35]. PGP-CDs are well dispersed and exhibit brownish-yellow color in visible light and intense blue-colored fluorescence in UV light irradiation (Fig. 3 a-inset). The fluorescence spectrum of the blue emitters shows that they exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission showing the bathochromic shift (redshift) from 430 nm to 530 nm upon exciting from 300 nm to 480 nm in the emission peak. PGP-CDs possess the maximum emission at 455 nm when excited at 380 nm (λ max) wavelength. The quantum yield of PGP-CDs in water was found to be 15% using quinine sulphate as reference [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect and stability of PGP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The variation in fluorescence properties of PGP-CDs was investigated at different pH ranging from 2 to 12. There is no significant impact upon the fluorescence intensity of PGP-CDs was observed, revealing the pH-independent emission of PGP-CDs (Fig. S3). This behavior could also be attributed to highly stable structural morphology of PGP-CDs which indicates the dominance of –CO and –OH groups as compared to easily ionized –NH2 and –COOH groups, etc. So, it can be proposed that the fluorescence-related surface states on the PGP-CDs are not significantly affected with the pH varying from acidic to alkaline [61]. Additionally, they show good photostability after continuous illumination of the UV- light of 365 nm for about 180 min (Fig. S5). Along with this, the fluorescence intensity of PGP-CDs displays almost negligible change when dispersed in 1.0 M NaCl, showing no aggregation at high ionic strengths. From this, it can be concluded that PGP-CDs are highly stable and are certain to have potential applications of these in varied fields (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopic studies of PGP-CDs with metal ions:
ce:section.ce:para:
PGP-CDs alone upon excitation at 380 nm show a strong emission band centered at 455 nm. The fluorescent behavior of the PGP-CDs upon addition of metal ions (1 × 10-5 M) like Ba(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Ca(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Mg(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), Hg(II), Pb(II), Sr(III), Zn(II), La(III) except Cr(VI) did not show any significant variation in the emission spectra (Fig. 4
a). However, the emission of PGP-CDs at 455 nm was selectively and strongly quenched by the addition of Cr(VI). Fluorescent quenching mechanism of PGP-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) ions was investigated by the help of UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 7
(a), the excitation spectrum of PGP-CDs has two bands at 260 and 320 nm, and the emission band of PGP-CDs under the excitation of 380 nm is centered at 455 nm; however, Cr(VI) exhibits broad absorption at 258, 350, and 440 nm, respectively, showing quite precise overlapping with the excitation bands of PGP-CDs. The plot reveals that there is a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber (Cr(VI)) and the excitation band of the fluorophore (PGP-CDs), while the emission spectrum of PGP-CDs is hardly affected by Cr(VI) ion. Thus, Cr(VI) can obscure adequate excitation light of PGP-CDs and weaken the emission light of PGP-CDs through the inner filter effect [51]. Fig. 7 (b,c) displays the schematic representation explaining the quenching mechanism i.e. IFE for PGP-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) ions. Furthermore, Fig. 4c showed the image of PGP-CDs irradiated under a UV lamp at 365 nm with different metal ions. Only Cr(VI) displays strong quenching (turn-off) under a UV lamp, whereas other metal ions display unquenched (turn-on) fluorescent emission. The same optimized experimental conditions have been followed for the synthesis of single and dual doped CDs viz. CDs with PEG only, P-CDs with PEG-400 + H3PO4, and NS-CDs using PEG-400 + glutathione as control group. Figure S9 (a) displays the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, P-CDs, NS-CDs and NSP-CDs (PGP-CDs). In the case of CDs and NS-CDs, no fluorescent product was obtained due to insufficient temperature for the reaction at optimized condition for the synthesis of NSP-CDs. However, a comparison of P-CDs and NSP-CDs with Cr(VI) has been displayed in Fig. S9 (b) to study the influence of N, P, and S doping on the sensing of Cr(VI).

To find out the quenching ability of the PGP-CDs towards Cr(VI), the fluorescence titration of nanoprobe in water with various concentrations of Cr(VI) was achieved in the range of 0–400 µM. As presented in Fig. 4b, the gradual addition of Cr(VI) (0–400 µM) results in a progressive decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak at 455 nm (λ max = 380 nm) and on further addition of higher concentration of Cr(VI) no change in the fluorescence intensity was noted. Notably, there was a good linear relationship between Io/I and the concentration of the Cr(VI) ion within the range of 0.1–100 µM (Fig. S6). The exponential equation was fitted to be Io/I = Ksv [Cr(VI)] (where Io and I are the fluorescence intensity of PGP-CDs before and after the addition of Cr(VI), respectively) with R2 = 0.9958. The limit of detection was found to be 0.04 × 10−7 M (4 nM) for Cr(VI) based on 3(SD)/Ksv (where SD is the standard deviation and Ksv is the slope of Stern-Volmer calibration plot). This LOD is comparable or lower than those of other reported fluorescent carbon dots showing better sensitivity of PGP-CDs (Table S1) [52,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of PGP-CDs towards Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the selectivity of PGP-CDs for Cr(VI), the competitive selectivity experiment in the presence of other metal ions (5 × 10-5 M) including Ba(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Ca(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Mg(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), Hg(II), Pb(II), Sr(III), Zn(II) and La(III) was accompanied at room temperature in water at pH 7 (Fig. 5). It is perceptible from the results attained that no considerable change was observed in the fluorescence intensity of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex at 455 nm upon excitation at 380 nm in the presence of other interfering metal ions. This analysis confirmed that the existence of other metal ions does not hinder the detection of Cr(VI), thus PGP-CDs can be credibly employed as a selective sensor for Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopic studies of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex with various biomolecules
ce:section.ce:para: Thereafter, the impact of different biomolecules on the dissociation of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex to regenerate PGP-CDs was explored. Fluorescence studies of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex were carried out in the presence of several biomolecules (1 × 10-5 M) including glycine (Gly), phenylalanine (Phl), proline (Pro), methionine (Met), histidine (His), ascorbic acid (AA), arginine (Arg), valine (Val), cysteine (Cys), serine (Ser), alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), sucrose (Suc), and dextrose (Dex). As represented in Fig. 6a, a momentous change was seen after the addition of AA to the solution containing the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex, while other biomolecules induced insignificant changes under identical conditions. Along with this, the quenched fluorescence of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) was complex restored upon the addition of AA only as shown in the photographic image under UV lamp (Fig. 6c).

For understanding the property of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex with interfering biomolecules, an aqueous solution of PGP-CDs having Cr(VI) was titrated with a gradual amount of AA. The fluorescence emission spectra of this experiment (Fig. 6b) exhibited an opposite trend from the titration plot attained with Cr(VI) (Fig. 4b), which indicates the revival of PGP-CDs from the complex (PGP-CD + Cr(VI)) in the presence of AA. The fluorescence titration technique was used to assess the fluorescence ‘on-off’ switching ability of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex. As depicted in Fig. 4b, on gradual addition of AA from 0 to 350 µM, the fluorescence of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) at 455 nm was regained progressively and no further alteration was observed in the intensity after the addition of 350 µM of AA. Thus, the results indicated that due to the larger affinity of AA towards Cr(VI), the PGP-CDs were revived in the process of detection of AA. The relative fluorescence intensities of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex show a linear relationship with the concentration of AA in the range of 1–105 μM. From the fluorescence emission titration spectra, the LOD of AA was calculated to be 0.5 × 10−7 M (50 nM), based on 3(SD)/Ksv (Fig. S7).

Reversibility, as well as regeneration of detecting nanoprobe, are vital factors in realistic applications. As seen in Fig. 8 , the sequential addition of the fixed amount of Cr(VI) and AA respectively to the PGP-CDs solution affords a variable change in fluorescence intensity at 455 nm (Fig. S8). The addition of AA results in the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) thus eliminating IFE as well as regaining the fluorescence of PGP-CDs (Fig. 7) [51]. This alterable fluorescence behavior of PGP-CDs can be reproduced up to four times by the alteration of Cr(VI)/AA addition. Thus, the PGP-CDs could be used as a switchable turn “on–off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for Cr(VI) and AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the viability of this fluorescent nanoprobe, tap water, drinking water, and discarded water from the textile industry of Jaipur, Rajasthan, India was collected and used to test the reliability and efficiency of the proposed system. The tap water, drinking water samples and discarded water samples from the textile industry (with pre-treatment) were collected and spiked with certain concentrations known of Cr(VI) and the results of the determination have been presented in Table 1 . Spiked recoveries were clearly in the range of 94%-106.5% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 2.16%. The results were similar to those found with the DPC method [54] for the detection of Cr(VI), and thus, the produced fluorescent probe was susceptible to Cr(VI). These findings suggested that the new approach has considerable potential for the determination of Cr(VI) in the water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in fruits and vegetables
ce:section.ce:para: Different fruits (kiwi, papaya) and vegetables (lemon, tomato, cucumber) with distinct AA levels were taken for investigation of concentration of AA to test the realistic applications of PGP-CD + Cr(VI) complex. The obtained AA content and % error with reference method in above real samples are represented in Table 2 . It should be noted that the concentration obtained from the proposed method was in good agreement with the reference method, i.e. OPDA method in which AA at pH 9.4 reacts with o-phenylenediamine to produce quinoxaline, a fluorescent compound [55]. Therefore, a nanosensor based on PGP-CD + Cr(VI) could be established as a promising platform for responsive and selective detection of AA in real samples.

The non-toxic nature of blue-emitting PGP-CDs at high concentrations indicates that it could potentially be used as a new form of biocompatible luminescent ink. PGP-CDs can be utilized as fluorescent ink without any chemical alteration. Fig. 9 displays the fluorescent characters written with these carbon dots on the Whatman filter paper when irradiated by a 365 nm UV beam. Therefore, it implies that the PGP-CDs ink can be a replacement for traditional fluorescent pens owing to its tremendous properties such as stable and persistent fluorescence, environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and easy to wash.

Depending upon the interaction and the consequent changes in the fluorescence intensity of PGP-CDs and its Cr(VI) complex with Cr(VI) and AA, respectively at 455 nm, we have used this ensemble to construct a combinatorial logic gate consisting of a special combination of logic functions NAND & NOT. Cr(VI) and AA acted as inputs and fluorescence emission at 455 nm operated as output. To illustrate the model of the logic gate, we assign logic 0 and logic 1 to the turn-off and turn-on fluorescence, respectively. Additionally, the viable four input sequences are (0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0) and (1, 1) as displayed in Fig. 10 . It has been noted that PGP-CDs stay turn-on in the absence of both inputs (Cr(VI) and AA). Moreover, input 1 i.e., Cr(VI) causes fluorescence quenching (turn-off) on interaction with the PGP-CDs procuring logic 0 while adding AA (input 2) activates the entire system resulting in output logic 1.

To summarize, a facile fluorescent assay using N-S-P co-doped carbon dots has been developed with amazing selectivity and sensitivity towards carcinogenic Cr(VI). Highly fluorescent, water-soluble, and blue light-emitting PGP-CDs were fabricated via simpler microwave-induced decomposition using glutathione, polyethylene glycol, and o-H3PO4 as the precursors. PGP-CDs exhibited pH-independent and excitation wavelength-dependent emission along with a quantum yield of ~15%. The CDs displayed blue fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima at 380/455 nm which can be turn-off by the add-on of Cr(VI) based on the IFE, followed by regaining of fluorescence on the sequential addition of AA. Thus, PGP-CDs act as a switchable turn “on–off-on sensor” for sequential detection of Cr(VI) and AA. The LOD was found to be 4 nM and 50 nM for Cr(VI) and AA respectively. Furthermore, this work has been extended to the construction of molecular logic gates based on the regaining of fluorescence of the PGP-CD + Cr(VI) solution in the presence of AA as well as the highly luminescent carbon dots also be served as fluorescent ink for writing purposes. Importantly, this “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor has been successfully employed to detect Cr(VI) and AA in real samples with good results. Based on outstanding performance, the current strategy offered a promising framework with a high degree of selectiveness and sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples at nano levels.

Harshita Laddha: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Priya Yadav: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yachana Jain: Visualization, Writing – original draft. Manish Sharma: Visualization. Mohtashim Reza: Investigation. Madhu Agarwal: Supervision, Visualization. Ragini Gupta: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Laddha is thankful to CSIR, New Delhi, for Junior Research Fellowship. P. Yadav acknowledges CSIR-UGC for the fellowship. M. Sharma acknowledges MNIT, Jaipur for financial assistance. The authors are grateful to the Materials Research Centre (MRC), MNIT Jaipur, for material characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.117088.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
